explanation_md: `Km(int)` is not marked `explicit`, so `trip(42)` silently converts `42` to `Km{42}` and prints `42km`. `trip(Km{10})` constructs explicitly and prints `10km`. Both calls succeed.

`explicit` only blocks implicit conversions and copy-initialization from a mismatched type. Direct-list initialization `Box b{5}` names the type explicitly and is always permitted. Copy initialization `Box b = 5` (option A) and passing a raw `int` to a function expecting `Box` (option C) are both implicit and are blocked. Copy-list initialization `Box b = {5}` (option D) is also blocked by `explicit`.

options.a: `explicit` has no effect on constructors with default arguments.
options.b: `explicit` only applies to copy constructors, not converting constructors.
options.c: The constructor can be called with one argument (because `channel` has a default), so the implicit conversion path still exists, and `explicit` should prevent `read(7)` — but there is a bug: `explicit` IS applied here, so `read(7)` should fail. The student may have a compiler that predates C++11.
options.d: `explicit` blocks direct-list initialization, not implicit calls through function parameters.

explanation_md: The student's understanding is actually correct: `explicit Sensor(int i, int channel = 0)` does block `read(7)` in C++11 and later. If the code compiled, the student is likely using a pre-C++11 compiler or a non-conforming mode where `explicit` on multi-parameter constructors was not yet enforced. The fix is to ensure the compiler is in C++11 mode or newer (`-std=c++20`). The `explicit` keyword applies to any single-argument-callable constructor regardless of default parameters.

options.a: It compiles fine; `explicit` has no effect on copy constructors.
options.b: It makes `b = a` a compile error because copy initialization is an implicit conversion.
options.c: It causes undefined behavior at runtime when copying.
options.d: It only breaks move construction, not copy construction.

explanation_md: `Widget b = a` is copy initialization, which uses the implicit conversion path. An `explicit` copy constructor blocks this, making even simple copy idioms fail. Standard containers, pass-by-value, and return-by-value all rely on implicit copy construction, so marking it `explicit` breaks almost everything. Copy constructors should virtually never be `explicit`.

`Flag{true}` is direct-list initialization — `explicit` does not block it. `show(f)` passes an existing `Flag` lvalue, so no conversion occurs. `show(Flag{false})` explicitly constructs a `Flag`. Both calls compile and print `on` then `off`.
